 `./.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl`

```text
1	#Requires -Version 7
2	# run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1 — install the toolset with scoop (CLI) +
3	# winget (GUI). Idempotent: only missing packages are installed. This script is
4	# the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for what gets installed; re-runs whenever the
5	# rendered content (i.e. any toggle below) changes.
6	#
7	# Toggles (from .chezmoi.toml.tmpl):
8	#   installCodingAgents  = {{ .installCodingAgents }}
9	#   installWindowsApps   = {{ .installWindowsApps }}
10	#   installUtilityApps   = {{ .installUtilityApps }}
11	#   installGamingApps    = {{ .installGamingApps }}
12	#   installExtraRuntimes = {{ .installExtraRuntimes }}
13	#   installMediaTools    = {{ .installMediaTools }}
14	#   installLlmTools      = {{ .installLlmTools }}
15	#   installTunnelTools   = {{ .installTunnelTools }}
16	#   installIacTools      = {{ .installIacTools }}
17	#   useChineseMirror     = {{ .useChineseMirror }}
18	#   managedMachine       = {{ .managedMachine }}
19	
20	# Resilient by design: individual package failures are collected and reported at
21	# the end, never fatal — one bad manifest must not abort the rest of the install
22	# (nor the rest of `chezmoi apply`). Mirrors ansible allow-partial-failure.
23	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'
24	if (Test-Path variable:PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference) { $PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $false }
25	$script:Failures = [System.Collections.Generic.List[string]]::new()
26	
27	# UTF-8 console so winget's localized output isn't mojibake on zh-* Windows.
28	try { [Console]::OutputEncoding = [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false) } catch { $null = $_ }
29	{{ if .useChineseMirror }}
30	# China (GFW) mirrors for install-time package fetches.
31	$env:PIP_INDEX_URL = 'https://pypi.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/simple'
32	$env:UV_DEFAULT_INDEX = 'https://pypi.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/simple'
33	$env:npm_config_registry = 'https://registry.npmmirror.com'
34	$env:GOPROXY = 'https://goproxy.cn,direct'
35	$env:MISE_NODE_MIRROR_URL = 'https://npmmirror.com/mirrors/node/'
36	{{ end }}
37	
38	function Info($m) { Write-Host "==> $m" -ForegroundColor Cyan }
39	function Have($n) { [bool](Get-Command $n -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) }
40	function Register-Failure($what) { $script:Failures.Add($what); Write-Warning "FAILED (skipped): $what" }
41	
42	# --- scoop -----------------------------------------------------------------
43	function Test-Admin {
44	    # $true when this pwsh is running elevated (member of the Administrators role).
45	    try {
46	        $id = [Security.Principal.WindowsIdentity]::GetCurrent()
47	        ([Security.Principal.WindowsPrincipal]$id).IsInRole([Security.Principal.WindowsBuiltInRole]::Administrator)
48	    } catch { $false }
49	}
50	
51	function Ensure-Scoop {
52	    if (-not (Have scoop)) {
53	        # get.scoop.sh returns install.ps1's text. Piping it to Invoke-Expression
54	        # works in a normal console, but an ELEVATED one is refused ("Running the
55	        # installer as administrator is disabled by default") unless -RunAsAdmin is
56	        # passed. Some dev boxes always open the shell as admin, so detect that and
57	        # forward the flag. We build a scriptblock from the fetched text so we can
58	        # pass args -- equivalent to `iex "& {$(irm get.scoop.sh)} -RunAsAdmin"`.
59	        $installer = [scriptblock]::Create((Invoke-RestMethod get.scoop.sh))
60	        if (Test-Admin) {
61	            Info 'scoop not found — installing (elevated console: passing -RunAsAdmin)'
62	            & $installer -RunAsAdmin
63	        } else {
64	            Info 'scoop not found — installing (user-scoped, no admin)'
65	            & $installer
66	        }
67	    }
68	    foreach ($b in 'main', 'extras', 'versions', 'nerd-fonts') {
69	        scoop bucket add $b *> $null
70	    }
71	}
72	
73	function Get-ScoopInstalled {
74	    try { return @((scoop export | ConvertFrom-Json).apps.name) } catch { return @() }
75	}
76	
77	function Scoop-Install([string[]]$apps) {
78	    if (-not $apps) { return }
79	    # Wrap in @() so a lone survivor stays an array. Without it PowerShell
80	    # unwraps a single match to a bare string, and `scoop install @missing`
81	    # then splats it one CHARACTER per arg => "Couldn't find manifest for 'm'"
82	    # when only "mise" was missing.
83	    $missing = @($apps | Where-Object { ($_ -split '/')[-1] -notin (Get-ScoopInstalled) })
84	    if (-not $missing) { Info 'scoop: all requested tools already present'; return }
85	    Info "scoop install: $($missing -join ', ')"
86	    try { scoop install @missing } catch { Write-Warning "scoop batch error: $_" }
87	    # Anything still absent after the batch = failed (scoop keeps going past a bad one).
88	    $after = Get-ScoopInstalled
89	    foreach ($app in $missing) {
90	        $name = ($app -split '/')[-1]
91	        if ($name -notin $after) { Register-Failure "scoop:$name" }
92	    }
93	}
94	
95	# --- winget ----------------------------------------------------------------
96	function Winget-Install([string]$id) {
97	    if (-not (Have winget)) { Register-Failure "winget:$id (winget unavailable)"; return }
98	    winget list --id $id -e --accept-source-agreements *> $null
99	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) { return }   # already installed
100	    Info "winget install $id"
101	    winget install --id $id -e --silent --scope user `
102	        --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements 2>&1 | Out-Host
103	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) {
104	        # some packages don't support --scope user; retry machine scope.
105	        winget install --id $id -e --silent `
106	            --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements 2>&1 | Out-Host
107	    }
108	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure "winget:$id" }
109	}
110	
111	# Microsoft Store packages (installed by Store product id via the msstore
112	# source) — used for apps with no community winget manifest, e.g. Raycast and the
113	# official ChatGPT desktop app. msstore ignores --scope, so we don't pass it.
114	function Winget-InstallStore([string]$id) {
115	    if (-not (Have winget)) { Register-Failure "msstore:$id (winget unavailable)"; return }
116	    winget list --id $id -e --accept-source-agreements *> $null
117	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) { return }   # already installed
118	    Info "winget (msstore) install $id"
119	    winget install --id $id -e --source msstore --silent `
120	        --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements 2>&1 | Out-Host
121	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure "msstore:$id" }
122	}
123	
124	# --- npm CLI agents --------------------------------------------------------
125	function Npm-InstallGlobal([string[]]$pkgs) {
126	    if (-not (Have npm)) { foreach ($p in $pkgs) { Register-Failure "npm:$p (npm unavailable)" }; return }
127	    foreach ($p in $pkgs) {
128	        Info "npm i -g $p"
129	        npm install -g $p 2>&1 | Out-Host
130	        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure "npm:$p" }
131	    }
132	}
133	
134	# ===========================================================================
135	Ensure-Scoop
136	
137	# Core CLI toolset — the shell baseline, always installed.
138	Scoop-Install @(
139	    'git', 'gh', 'glab', '7zip', 'make', 'just',
140	    'starship', 'zoxide', 'fzf', 'fd', 'ripgrep', 'bat', 'eza', 'jq',
141	    'delta', 'lazygit', 'neovim', 'yazi', 'btop', 'television', 'tlrc',
142	    'uv', 'gnupg',
143	    # Runtimes are NATIVE via scoop, not mise: mise's activate/shims are
144	    # unreliable on Windows and it has no windows-arm64 bun build. node + bun
145	    # here; Python via uv (below); go/rust/ruby under Extra runtimes.
146	    # copilot-proxy needs bun/bunx.
147	    'nodejs-lts', 'bun',
148	    # LazyVim needs a C compiler for tree-sitter parsers (zig), the tree-sitter
149	    # CLI the nvim-treesitter `main` branch requires, and a clipboard provider so
150	    # `unnamedplus` yanks reach the Windows clipboard (win32yank).
151	    'zig', 'tree-sitter', 'win32yank'
152	)
153	
154	# On Windows-on-ARM, scoop's neovim is amd64 (runs under x64 emulation) while
155	# zig installs arm64-native, so zig-built tree-sitter parsers are the wrong
156	# arch for nvim. Add an amd64 gcc that matches the emulated nvim.
157	if ([System.Runtime.InteropServices.RuntimeInformation]::OSArchitecture -eq 'Arm64') {
158	    Info 'ARM64 host: adding amd64 gcc for nvim-treesitter'
159	    Scoop-Install @('gcc')
160	}
161	
162	# gh-dash: GitHub PR/issue dashboard TUI (`gh dash`). It's a gh extension, not a
163	# scoop package, so it needs gh to be authenticated -- this no-ops on a fresh
164	# machine until `gh auth login`. run_onchange only re-fires when this script
165	# changes, so after authenticating, install by hand (`gh extension install
166	# dlvhdr/gh-dash`) or force a reapply (`chezmoi state delete-bucket
167	# --bucket=entryState`). gh-notify / gh-select from the Unix repo are bash-only
168	# extensions and are intentionally skipped on Windows.
169	if (Have gh) {
170	    gh auth status *> $null
171	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) {
172	        $ghExts = gh extension list 2>$null | Out-String
173	        if ($ghExts -notmatch 'dlvhdr/gh-dash') {
174	            Info 'gh extension install dlvhdr/gh-dash'
175	            gh extension install dlvhdr/gh-dash
176	            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure 'gh-ext:gh-dash' }
177	        }
178	    } else {
179	        Info 'gh not authenticated -- skipping gh-dash (gh auth login, then reapply)'
180	    }
181	}
182	
183	# node/bun come from scoop above (on ~/scoop/shims, reliably on PATH). We do NOT
184	# use mise here -- see the runtimes note in the core scoop list.
185	
186	# Default Python, uv-managed. --default also creates unversioned python/python3
187	# (not just python3.x) in UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR (~/.local/bin, on PATH via 00_env
188	# ahead of the Store's python.exe app-alias). On uv 0.8+ `--default` lives behind
189	# preview mode, so it must be paired with `--preview` (older uv just ignored it) —
190	# without it, current uv errors: "`--default` is only available in preview mode".
191	if (Have uv) {
192	    Info 'uv python install --default --preview'
193	    $env:UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR = Join-Path $HOME '.local\bin'
194	    uv python install --default --preview 2>&1 | Out-Host
195	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure 'uv:python' }
196	}
197	
198	# Make PSGallery usable non-interactively before Install-Module (NuGet provider
199	# + trust) so the fzf key bindings / audio cmdlets install reliably.
200	try {
201	    if (-not (Get-PackageProvider -Name NuGet -ListAvailable -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) {
202	        Install-PackageProvider -Name NuGet -Scope CurrentUser -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Out-Null
203	    }
204	    if ((Get-PSRepository -Name PSGallery -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).InstallationPolicy -ne 'Trusted') {
205	        Set-PSRepository -Name PSGallery -InstallationPolicy Trusted -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
206	    }
207	} catch { Write-Warning "PSGallery prep failed (non-fatal): $_" }

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm seeing the installer architecture more clearly now. There's a pattern with `irm | iex` bootstrap installs like `Ensure-Scoop` that fetches and executes scripts, and the herdr installer follows the same approach. Toggles get declared in `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` using `promptBoolOnce`, with a header comment in the packages script documenting them, and failures are tracked through `Register-Failure`.

For herdr specifically, it's a terminal multiplexer that doesn't fit neatly into existing toggles—it's not quite a GUI app like VSCode or WezTerm, and it's too experimental to force on everyone. A dedicated opt-in toggle (`installHerdr`) defaulting to `false` makes the most sense and mirrors the pattern used for `installSshServer` and `installLlmTools`. This approach respects that herdr is beta, Windows-preview-only, and unsigned.

Adding a new toggle means updating multiple files to maintain consistency: `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` gets the new `promptBoolOnce`, and `.github/workflows/windows.yml` needs corresponding changes.

I'm waiting for the explore agent to finish mapping out the CI workflow pattern and documentation structure so I can get exact locations and formatting for the setup, tools, and rationale docs, plus confirm how `00_env.ps1` handles PATH prepending.

Meanwhile, I'm reconsidering whether herdr needs its own dedicated toggle or if it should nest under an existing category like `installWindowsApps`—there's a trade-off between keeping it opt-in and clean versus avoiding extra wiring, though herdr isn't really a GUI app or winget-installable like the others in that group.

I'm leaning toward a new `installHerdr` toggle to stay consistent with how the repo handles other optional/experimental tools, even though it means more cross-file changes. But I should probably ask the user about this design choice before committing to the wiring work.

There's also the question of whether to ship a managed herdr config file — I'm thinking install-only for now, with config management as a follow-up, though I need to confirm the exact config path. For the shell integration, I'm wondering whether to include a fragment that sets up herdr automatically.

Looking at the PATH situation, the installer updates the User PATH persistently but that only takes effect in new shells. Since `00_env.ps1` already prepends known directories for immediate availability, adding herdr's bin directory there would be a nice touch for same-session access. Let me check what's already in that file and see if I should add it.
</details></think>

1	# 00_env.ps1 — environment variables & PATH. Sourced first by $PROFILE.
2	
3	# UTF-8 console I/O. Without this, native tools that emit UTF-8 (git, winget's
4	# localized output, …) render as mojibake on legacy-codepage systems (e.g. GBK /
5	# cp936 on zh-* Windows). Guarded so a redirected/headless host can't error the
6	# rest of this fragment.
7	try {
8	    $utf8 = [System.Text.UTF8Encoding]::new($false)
9	    [Console]::OutputEncoding = $utf8
10	    $OutputEncoding = $utf8
11	} catch { $null = $_ }
12	
13	# XDG-style base dirs. Several tools honor these on Windows (starship, atuin,
14	# zoxide state, our own copilot module) so we set them for a tidy $HOME.
15	if (-not $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME) { $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $HOME '.config' }
16	if (-not $env:XDG_DATA_HOME)   { $env:XDG_DATA_HOME   = Join-Path $HOME '.local/share' }
17	if (-not $env:XDG_STATE_HOME)  { $env:XDG_STATE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.local/state' }
18	if (-not $env:XDG_CACHE_HOME)  { $env:XDG_CACHE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.cache' }
19	
20	$env:STARSHIP_CONFIG = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'starship.toml'
21	# yazi looks in %APPDATA%\yazi\config by default; keep it XDG-consistent instead.
22	$env:YAZI_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'yazi'
23	
24	# yazi needs the MSYS `file.exe` (bundled with Git) for MIME detection; without
25	# it every entry errors "Cannot find `file` … Set it up per the Windows guide".
26	# scoop doesn't shim git's usr/bin, so point YAZI_FILE_ONE at file.exe directly;
27	# fall back to a system/winget Git install. First existing candidate wins.
28	if (-not $env:YAZI_FILE_ONE) {
29	    $fileOne = @(
30	        (Join-Path $HOME 'scoop/apps/git/current/usr/bin/file.exe'),
31	        'C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin\file.exe'
32	    ) | Where-Object { Test-Path -LiteralPath $_ } | Select-Object -First 1
33	    if ($fileOne) { $env:YAZI_FILE_ONE = $fileOne }
34	}
35	
36	# uv-managed Python: `uv python install --default` drops python/python3 here.
37	# ~/.local/bin is prepended to PATH below, so they win over the Windows Store
38	# `python.exe` app-execution-alias.
39	$env:UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR = Join-Path $HOME '.local/bin'
40	
41	# Default editor
42	if (Get-Command nvim -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) { $env:EDITOR = 'nvim' }
43	
44	# Prepend user bin dirs to PATH, idempotently. scoop already adds its shims to
45	# the persisted user PATH, but a fresh session inside chezmoi apply may not have
46	# it yet; ~/.local/bin carries uv tools + the uv-managed python. Normalize each
47	# entry to backslashes (mixed slashes can fail Windows command resolution).
48	$UserPaths = @(
49	    (Join-Path $HOME '.local/bin'),

# herdr integration — repo exploration report

Note up front: this repo already contains a SpecStory transcript about herdr at `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.specstory/history/2026-07-11_00-08-14Z-herdr.md`. It records that **herdr has no scoop/winget/npm manifest** — it installs via an `irm | iex` script: `irm https://herdr.dev/install.ps1 | iex` (defaults to the `preview` channel; bin junction at `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin`; releases under `%USERPROFILE%\.herdr\packages\standalone\releases`; GitHub `ogulcancelik/herdr`). That shapes the plan: herdr follows the same `irm | iex` shape as scoop's own installer, not the scoop/winget/npm paths. Details below with the exact blocks you'd mirror.

---

## 1. Package installer — `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl`

Absolute path: `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl`

**Header toggle manifest (lines 7–18)** — every toggle is echoed here so `run_onchange` re-fires when any flips. You'd add an `installHerdr` line here:
```
# Toggles (from .chezmoi.toml.tmpl):
#   installCodingAgents  = {{ .installCodingAgents }}
#   installWindowsApps   = {{ .installWindowsApps }}
#   ...
#   installIacTools      = {{ .installIacTools }}
#   useChineseMirror     = {{ .useChineseMirror }}
#   managedMachine       = {{ .managedMachine }}
```

**Failure collection + helpers (lines 23–40)** — the `Register-Failure` mechanism:
```powershell
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'
if (Test-Path variable:PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference) { $PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $false }
$script:Failures = [System.Collections.Generic.List[string]]::new()
...
function Info($m) { Write-Host "==> $m" -ForegroundColor Cyan }
function Have($n) { [bool](Get-Command $n -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) }
function Register-Failure($what) { $script:Failures.Add($what); Write-Warning "FAILED (skipped): $what" }
```

**scoop install path (lines 42–93)** — the closest structural precedent for herdr, because `Ensure-Scoop` uses the same `irm | iex` install-via-fetched-scriptblock pattern herdr needs (lines 51–71):
```powershell
function Ensure-Scoop {
    if (-not (Have scoop)) {
        $installer = [scriptblock]::Create((Invoke-RestMethod get.scoop.sh))
        if (Test-Admin) {
            Info 'scoop not found — installing (elevated console: passing -RunAsAdmin)'
            & $installer -RunAsAdmin
        } else {
            Info 'scoop not found — installing (user-scoped, no admin)'
            & $installer
        }
    }
    foreach ($b in 'main', 'extras', 'versions', 'nerd-fonts') {
        scoop bucket add $b *> $null
    }
}
```
And the batch installer with per-package failure detection (lines 77–93):
```powershell
function Scoop-Install([string[]]$apps) {
    if (-not $apps) { return }
    $missing = @($apps | Where-Object { ($_ -split '/')[-1] -notin (Get-ScoopInstalled) })
    if (-not $missing) { Info 'scoop: all requested tools already present'; return }
    Info "scoop install: $($missing -join ', ')"
    try { scoop install @missing } catch { Write-Warning "scoop batch error: $_" }
    $after = Get-ScoopInstalled
    foreach ($app in $missing) {
        $name = ($app -split '/')[-1]
        if ($name -notin $after) { Register-Failure "scoop:$name" }
    }
}
```

**winget install path (lines 96–122)** — `$LASTEXITCODE`-gated, user-scope then machine-scope retry, plus a Store variant:
```powershell
function Winget-Install([string]$id) {
    if (-not (Have winget)) { Register-Failure "winget:$id (winget unavailable)"; return }
    winget list --id $id -e --accept-source-agreements *> $null
    if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) { return }   # already installed
    Info "winget install $id"
    winget install --id $id -e --silent --scope user `
        --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements 2>&1 | Out-Host
    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) {
        winget install --id $id -e --silent `
            --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements 2>&1 | Out-Host
    }
    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure "winget:$id" }
}
```

**npm global path (lines 125–132)**:
```powershell
function Npm-InstallGlobal([string[]]$pkgs) {
    if (-not (Have npm)) { foreach ($p in $pkgs) { Register-Failure "npm:$p (npm unavailable)" }; return }
    foreach ($p in $pkgs) {
        Info "npm i -g $p"
        npm install -g $p 2>&1 | Out-Host
        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure "npm:$p" }
    }
}
```

**PSGallery path (lines 199–219)** — NuGet provider + trust prep, then `Install-Module` guarded by `Get-Module -ListAvailable`:
```powershell
if (-not (Get-Module -ListAvailable -Name PSFzf)) {
    Info 'Install-Module PSFzf'
    Install-Module PSFzf -Scope CurrentUser -Force -AcceptLicense -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
}
```

**How each installer is gated by an `installX` toggle** — the templated blocks near the bottom (lines 221–333). Every optional group is a `{{ if .installX -}} … {{ end -}}` fence. The `installTunnelTools` block (lines 317–319) is the simplest, cleanest template to mirror for a scoop-style tool:
```powershell
{{ if .installTunnelTools -}}
Scoop-Install @('ngrok', 'cloudflared')
{{ end -}}
```
The `installLlmTools` block (lines 311–315) is the closest precedent for a **non-scoop/non-winget** tool that shells out to a one-off command with a `$LASTEXITCODE` failure check — mirror this shape for herdr's `irm | iex`:
```powershell
{{ if .installLlmTools -}}
Winget-Install 'Ollama.Ollama'
if (Have uv) { Info 'uv tool install litellm'; uv tool install 'litellm[proxy]'; if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure 'uv:litellm' } }
{{ end -}}
```

**Failure summary footer (lines 335–341)**:
```powershell
if ($script:Failures.Count -gt 0) {
    Write-Warning ("package install: {0} item(s) failed and were skipped:" -f $script:Failures.Count)
    $script:Failures | ForEach-Object { Write-Warning "  - $_" }
    Write-Warning "Re-run 'just apply' (or 'chezmoi apply') to retry, or install them manually."
} else {
    Info 'package install complete — no failures'
}
```

For herdr you'd add a new helper function (mirroring `Ensure-Scoop`'s scriptblock-from-`Invoke-RestMethod` idiom + `Have` guard + `Register-Failure`), e.g. an `Install-Herdr` placed alongside the other `--- xxx ---` function sections (around lines 124–132), then a `{{ if .installHerdr -}} Install-Herdr {{ end -}}` fence in the bottom block region.

## 3. CI — `.github/workflows/windows.yml`

Absolute path: `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.github/workflows/windows.yml`

The non-interactive init step (lines 44–66). Each answer is passed as `--promptBool`/`--promptChoice`/`--promptString` with a `Prompt text=value` pair — the text must match `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` exactly:
```powershell
- name: chezmoi init (writes config from prompt answers)
  shell: pwsh
  run: |
    $flags = @(
      '--promptChoice','Role: workstation (full desktop) or minimal (shell only)=minimal',
      '--promptString','Your full name (git)=CI Runner',
      '--promptString','Your git email=ci@example.com',
      '--promptBool','Install coding agents (Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex, Copilot CLI, SpecStory)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install GUI apps via winget (VSCode, Cursor, Notepad++, Terminal, Alacritty, PowerToys, Raycast)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install utility apps via winget (CPU-Z, GPU-Z, TreeSize, VLC, Everything, ShareX, HWiNFO)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install gaming apps (Steam)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install extra runtimes (rust, go, ruby) beyond node/bun/uv=false',
      '--promptBool','Install media CLIs (ffmpeg, imagemagick)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install local LLM tools (Ollama, LiteLLM)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install tunnel tools (ngrok, cloudflared)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install Infrastructure-as-Code tools (Azure CLI, Terraform, OpenTofu)=false',
      '--promptBool','Install and enable the OpenSSH server / sshd (needs admin, opens inbound port 22)=false',
      '--promptBool','Use China (GFW) mirrors for pip/npm/cargo/go/node=false',
      '--promptBool','Managed or corporate machine (skip org-policy-blocked apps like Tailscale and Grammarly)=false',
      '--promptChoice','Back up existing files before apply (smart|full|off)=off',
      '--promptBool','Enable PSReadLine vi editing mode=true'
    )
    chezmoi init --source $PWD --no-tty @flags
```

```
'--promptBool','Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)=false',
```
Note: chezmoi `--no-tty` requires an answer for **every** prompt, so this line is mandatory or the whole CI init step blocks/fails. Two later steps also exercise the rendered script: "Parse-check rendered .ps1.tmpl scripts" (lines 68–80, renders + PowerShell-parses every `*.ps1.tmpl`) and "chezmoi apply (files only, `--exclude scripts`)" (lines 82–89) — so a herdr fragment/script must render and parse clean, but the installer itself won't actually run in CI (scripts are excluded).

```powershell
if (Test-Path $FragmentDir) {
    foreach ($fragment in Get-ChildItem -Path $FragmentDir -Filter '*.ps1' | Sort-Object Name) {
        try { . $fragment.FullName }
        catch { Write-Warning "profile.d: failed to load $($fragment.Name): $_" }
    }
}
```

**Representative activation fragment** — `35_yazi.ps1` (the whole file), the canonical "no-op if the tool is absent" pattern a herdr fragment would mirror:
```powershell
# 35_yazi.ps1 — `y` launches yazi and cd's to wherever you quit ...
if (Get-Command yazi -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) {
    function y {
        $tmp = New-TemporaryFile
        try {
            yazi @args --cwd-file $tmp.FullName
            ...
        } finally {
            Remove-Item -LiteralPath $tmp.FullName -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
        }
    }
}
```
Compare `10_tools.ps1` for tools that emit a shell-`init` hook (starship/zoxide use the cached `Import-CachedInit` helper, lines 10–37). herdr likely needs neither an init-hook nor an alias — its main integration is **PATH** (its bin isn't under `~/scoop/shims`), which belongs in `00_env.ps1` (see item 5). If herdr does emit shell integration, `Import-CachedInit -Name 'herdr' -Exe 'herdr' -Generate { herdr init powershell }` (10_tools.ps1 lines 10–40) is the pattern.

Templated fragments (needed if a fragment body must be gated by `installHerdr`): `05_mirrors.ps1.tmpl` shows the `{{ if .X -}} … {{ end -}}` body-gate, and `90_psreadline.ps1.tmpl` shows mid-file `{{- if .X }}`. A `Get-Command herdr` guard usually makes gating unnecessary (fragment self-no-ops when herdr isn't installed), which is the established convention here.

## 5. scoop bucket configuration

There is no separate bucket file — buckets are added imperatively inside `Ensure-Scoop` in the packages script, `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl` lines 68–70:
```powershell
    foreach ($b in 'main', 'extras', 'versions', 'nerd-fonts') {
        scoop bucket add $b *> $null
    }
```
Bucket-qualified installs use a `bucket/app` string, e.g. line 246 `Scoop-Install @('extras/opencode-desktop')`; `Scoop-Install` strips the prefix via `($_ -split '/')[-1]` for its installed-check (lines 83, 90). So if herdr ever lands in a scoop bucket you'd add the bucket name to that `foreach` list and install `bucket/herdr`. As of now (per the herdr transcript) **herdr has no scoop manifest**, so this path likely doesn't apply — herdr is an `irm | iex` installer.

**PATH note (important for herdr):** herdr's binary junction is `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin`, not `~/scoop/shims`. The PATH-prepend block lives in `00_env.ps1` lines 44–57:
```powershell
$UserPaths = @(
    (Join-Path $HOME '.local/bin'),
    (Join-Path $HOME 'scoop/shims')
) | Where-Object { $_ -and (Test-Path $_) } |
    ForEach-Object { [System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath($_) } | Select-Object -Unique

$SepPaths = $env:PATH -split ';'
foreach ($p in $UserPaths) {
    if ($SepPaths -notcontains $p) { $env:PATH = "$p;$env:PATH" }
}
```
You'd add `(Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA 'Programs\Herdr\bin')` to the `$UserPaths` array so `herdr` resolves in interactive shells (the `Test-Path` filter makes it a clean no-op when herdr isn't installed).

```md
# Add herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)

## Context

`docs/rationale.md:64-83` currently asserts there is **no native Windows terminal
multiplexer** — tmux/zellij are Unix-only, so WezTerm is used as the closest
answer. [herdr](https://herdr.dev) has since shipped an **experimental Windows
beta**: a mouse-first, native (ConPTY-based) terminal multiplexer with panes,
splits, persistent sessions, and detach/attach — exactly the gap that section
describes.

Goal: install herdr as an **opt-in** tool and manage its config, wiring it
through the repo the same way every other optional tool is wired.

**Locked decisions (confirmed with user):**
1. **Gating** — a new dedicated `installHerdr` init toggle, default `false`
   (herdr is beta / preview-only / unsigned; opt-in only).
2. **Config** — the dotfiles manage herdr's `config.toml` as well as installing
   the binary.

**Key facts driving the design:**
- herdr has **no scoop/winget/npm manifest** — it installs via an `irm | iex`
  bootstrap: `irm https://herdr.dev/install.ps1 | iex`. This is the same shape as
  this repo's own `Ensure-Scoop` (`run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl:51-71`),
  so it needs a bespoke `Install-Herdr` helper, not the scoop/winget/npm paths.
- The installer is **non-interactive, user-scoped, no elevation**, idempotent
  (lock file, skips re-download). Windows is **preview-only** (honors
  `$env:HERDR_CHANNEL`). Bin junction lands at `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin`
  (not `~/scoop/shims`), which it prepends to User PATH.
- The `install.ps1` itself writes **no** config file → no installer-vs-chezmoi
  drift; chezmoi can fully own the config.
- herdr config is TOML at `%APPDATA%\herdr\config.toml` by default, but honors
  **`HERDR_CONFIG_PATH`** to relocate it — a clean fit for this repo's XDG
  pattern (identical to `STARSHIP_CONFIG` in `00_env.ps1:20`). Notable non-default
  keys: `[update] channel` must be `preview` on Windows; `[terminal] default_shell`
  defaults to `"nu"` — this repo standardizes on `pwsh` (matches WezTerm).

## Changes

### 1. Install wiring — `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl`

- **Header toggle manifest** (after line 16, in the `# Toggles` block): add
  `#   installHerdr         = {{ .installHerdr }}`.
- **New helper**, placed alongside the other `--- xxx ---` function sections
  (e.g. after the npm block, ~line 132). Models `Ensure-Scoop` (fetch script →
  scriptblock → invoke), guarded so it's a no-op when already present, failures
  via `Register-Failure`, checks the real install path (independent of PATH,
  which won't refresh mid-apply):

  \```powershell
  # --- herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta) -------------
  # No scoop/winget/npm manifest — installs via herdr.dev's irm|iex bootstrap
  # (same shape as Ensure-Scoop). Windows is preview-only; the bin junction is
  # %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin (added to PATH in profile.d/00_env.ps1).
  function Install-Herdr {
      $herdrBin = Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA 'Programs\Herdr\bin\herdr.exe'
      if ((Have herdr) -or (Test-Path -LiteralPath $herdrBin)) { Info 'herdr already installed'; return }
      Info 'installing herdr (preview) via herdr.dev/install.ps1'
      try {
          $env:HERDR_CHANNEL = 'preview'   # Windows builds are preview-only
          $installer = [scriptblock]::Create((Invoke-RestMethod https://herdr.dev/install.ps1))
          & $installer
          if (-not (Test-Path -LiteralPath $herdrBin)) { Register-Failure 'herdr' }
      } catch {
          Write-Warning "herdr install error: $_"
          Register-Failure 'herdr'
      }
  }
  \```

- **Gated call** in the bottom `{{ if .installX -}}` region (mirror the
  `installTunnelTools` block, ~line 317):

  \```
  {{ if .installHerdr -}}
  Install-Herdr
  {{ end -}}
  \```

### 2. Init prompt — `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl`

Add after `installSshServer` (line 27). **The prompt TEXT is the CI join key —
copy it verbatim into `windows.yml` (invariant #1); no `=` in the text.**

\```toml
    installHerdr         = {{ promptBoolOnce . "installHerdr" "Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)" false }}
\```

### 3. CI flags — `.github/workflows/windows.yml`

Add to the `$flags` array (after the sshd line, ~line 60), text matching #2 exactly:

\```
      '--promptBool','Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)=false',
\```

### 4. PATH + config env — `dot_config/powershell/profile.d/00_env.ps1`

- Add herdr's bin to the `$UserPaths` array (lines 48-51) so `herdr` resolves in
  interactive shells immediately; the existing `Where-Object { Test-Path }`
  filter makes it a clean no-op when herdr isn't installed:

  \```powershell
      (Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA 'Programs\Herdr\bin'),
  \```

- Add `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH` next to the other XDG file-redirects (near line 20-22,
  by `STARSHIP_CONFIG`):

  \```powershell
  # herdr reads its config here instead of %APPDATA%\herdr\config.toml (XDG-tidy).
  $env:HERDR_CONFIG_PATH = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'herdr/config.toml'
  \```

### 5. Managed config — new file `dot_config/herdr/config.toml`

Static TOML (no templating needed), deployed to `~/.config/herdr/config.toml`:

\```toml
# herdr — native Windows terminal multiplexer (preview beta).
# Managed by chezmoi; read via $env:HERDR_CONFIG_PATH (profile.d/00_env.ps1).
# Full default: `herdr --default-config`. Docs: https://herdr.dev/docs/configuration/

[update]
# Windows builds are preview-only; the installer rejects "stable" here.
channel = "preview"

[terminal]
# Match the rest of this repo (WezTerm / Windows Terminal default to pwsh).
default_shell = "pwsh"

[ui]
# "auto" draws herdr's own cursor on Windows to avoid ConPTY flicker;
# set "native" to hand the cursor to the outer terminal.
host_cursor = "auto"
\```

### 6. Gate the config file — `.chezmoiignore`

`.chezmoiignore` ignores `docs/**` etc. but not `.config/**`, so the file would
deploy for everyone. `.chezmoiignore` is rendered as a Go template, so gate it on
the toggle (append):

\```
{{ if not .installHerdr }}
.config/herdr/config.toml
{{ end }}
\```

### 7. Docs + skill (bilingual mirrors, same commit)

- **`docs/rationale.md:64-83` + `docs/rationale.zh-TW.md:55-71`** — update the
  multiplexer section: add a **herdr** row to the comparison table (native
  Windows, splits/tabs + detach/persist, "beta/preview") and a short paragraph
  noting herdr is now the native tmux-like option (opt-in via `installHerdr`)
  while **WezTerm stays the stable default**. Soften the "no native Windows
  builds" framing so it no longer reads as absolute.
- **`docs/tools.md:125-134` + `docs/tools.zh-TW.md:121-130`** — add a row to the
  "Opt-in dev stacks" table:
  `| herdr | native Windows terminal multiplexer (preview beta), installed via herdr.dev's install script; config at ~/.config/herdr/config.toml |`
- **`docs/setup.md:31-48` + `docs/setup.zh-TW.md:26-42`** — add an init-prompt
  table row: `| herdr multiplexer | off | native Windows terminal multiplexer (preview beta) |`
- **`.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md`** — add `· herdr: {{ .installHerdr }}`
  to the "What's enabled" line (line 56) and update the tmux/zellij Gotcha
  (lines 69-71) to mention herdr as the native beta multiplexer.
- **`CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` "Config surfaces" line** (~line 54) — append herdr
  (`~/.config/herdr/config.toml` via `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH`) so the architecture map
  stays accurate. Minor; keeps future agents oriented.

## Files to change

| # | File | Change |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` | new `installHerdr` prompt (default false) |
| 2 | `.github/workflows/windows.yml` | matching `--promptBool` flag |
| 3 | `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl` | header note + `Install-Herdr` + gated call |
| 4 | `dot_config/powershell/profile.d/00_env.ps1` | PATH entry + `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH` |
| 5 | `dot_config/herdr/config.toml` | **new** managed config |
| 6 | `.chezmoiignore` | gate config on `installHerdr` |
| 7 | `docs/rationale.md` + `.zh-TW.md` | multiplexer section update |
| 8 | `docs/tools.md` + `.zh-TW.md` | opt-in dev stacks row |
| 9 | `docs/setup.md` + `.zh-TW.md` | init-prompt table row |
| 10 | `.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md` | "What's enabled" + Gotcha |
| 11 | `CLAUDE.md` (`AGENTS.md` symlink) | config-surfaces line |

## Verification

Off-Windows (this checkout) — the installer can't actually run here; the
`windows-latest` CI is the real gate. Validate structurally:

1. **Template renders + parses** (isolated apply idiom from CLAUDE.md). Init a
   throwaway state passing **every** prompt including the new one
   (`--promptBool 'Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)=true'`),
   then `chezmoi execute-template … < run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl > r.ps1`
   and parse with pwsh — confirm the `Install-Herdr` block is present and parses.
   Re-render with the flag `=false` and confirm the block is absent.
2. **Config gating** — with `installHerdr=false`, confirm `chezmoi apply
   --exclude=scripts` into the temp destination does **not** write
   `.config/herdr/config.toml`; with `=true`, confirm it does.
3. **Config validity** — parse `dot_config/herdr/config.toml` with Python
   `tomllib` (per invariant #3 spirit) to confirm valid TOML.
4. **Lint** — `Invoke-ScriptAnalyzer` on the changed `.ps1.tmpl` (Errors only).
5. **Docs** — `just docs-build` must stay green (`--strict`; bilingual twins).
6. **CI parity** — confirm the new `windows.yml` flag text is byte-identical to
   the `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` prompt text (otherwise `--no-tty` init hangs).

On a real Windows box (post-merge sanity): `chezmoi apply` with herdr enabled →
`herdr --version` resolves, new shell has `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin` on
PATH, and `herdr` reads `~/.config/herdr/config.toml` (pwsh as default shell).

## Out of scope (note as TODO if wanted later)

- A `profile.d/15_herdr.ps1` shell-integration fragment (herdr's live-cwd is
  handled by `[terminal] new_cwd`, and PATH/config are covered in `00_env.ps1`)
  — add only if herdr ships a `herdr init powershell` hook worth caching.

# Add herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)

## Context

`docs/rationale.md:64-83` currently asserts there is **no native Windows terminal
multiplexer** — tmux/zellij are Unix-only, so WezTerm is used as the closest
answer. [herdr](https://herdr.dev) has since shipped an **experimental Windows
beta**: a mouse-first, native (ConPTY-based) terminal multiplexer with panes,
splits, persistent sessions, and detach/attach — exactly the gap that section
describes.

Goal: install herdr as an **opt-in** tool and manage its config, wiring it
through the repo the same way every other optional tool is wired.

**Locked decisions (confirmed with user):**
1. **Gating** — a new dedicated `installHerdr` init toggle, default `false`
   (herdr is beta / preview-only / unsigned; opt-in only).
2. **Config** — the dotfiles manage herdr's `config.toml` as well as installing
   the binary.

**Key facts driving the design:**
- herdr has **no scoop/winget/npm manifest** — it installs via an `irm | iex`
  bootstrap: `irm https://herdr.dev/install.ps1 | iex`. This is the same shape as
  this repo's own `Ensure-Scoop` (`run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl:51-71`),
  so it needs a bespoke `Install-Herdr` helper, not the scoop/winget/npm paths.
- The installer is **non-interactive, user-scoped, no elevation**, idempotent
  (lock file, skips re-download). Windows is **preview-only** (honors
  `$env:HERDR_CHANNEL`). Bin junction lands at `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin`
  (not `~/scoop/shims`), which it prepends to User PATH.
- The `install.ps1` itself writes **no** config file → no installer-vs-chezmoi
  drift; chezmoi can fully own the config.
- herdr config is TOML at `%APPDATA%\herdr\config.toml` by default, but honors
  **`HERDR_CONFIG_PATH`** to relocate it — a clean fit for this repo's XDG
  pattern (identical to `STARSHIP_CONFIG` in `00_env.ps1:20`). Notable non-default
  keys: `[update] channel` must be `preview` on Windows; `[terminal] default_shell`
  defaults to `"nu"` — this repo standardizes on `pwsh` (matches WezTerm).

## Changes

### 1. Install wiring — `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl`

- **Header toggle manifest** (after line 16, in the `# Toggles` block): add
  `#   installHerdr         = {{ .installHerdr }}`.
- **New helper**, placed alongside the other `--- xxx ---` function sections
  (e.g. after the npm block, ~line 132). Models `Ensure-Scoop` (fetch script →
  scriptblock → invoke), guarded so it's a no-op when already present, failures
  via `Register-Failure`, checks the real install path (independent of PATH,
  which won't refresh mid-apply):

  \```powershell
  # --- herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta) -------------
  # No scoop/winget/npm manifest — installs via herdr.dev's irm|iex bootstrap
  # (same shape as Ensure-Scoop). Windows is preview-only; the bin junction is
  # %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin (added to PATH in profile.d/00_env.ps1).
  function Install-Herdr {
      $herdrBin = Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA 'Programs\Herdr\bin\herdr.exe'
      if ((Have herdr) -or (Test-Path -LiteralPath $herdrBin)) { Info 'herdr already installed'; return }
      Info 'installing herdr (preview) via herdr.dev/install.ps1'
      try {
          $env:HERDR_CHANNEL = 'preview'   # Windows builds are preview-only
          $installer = [scriptblock]::Create((Invoke-RestMethod https://herdr.dev/install.ps1))
          & $installer
          if (-not (Test-Path -LiteralPath $herdrBin)) { Register-Failure 'herdr' }
      } catch {
          Write-Warning "herdr install error: $_"
          Register-Failure 'herdr'
      }
  }
  \```

- **Gated call** in the bottom `{{ if .installX -}}` region (mirror the
  `installTunnelTools` block, ~line 317):

  \```
  {{ if .installHerdr -}}
  Install-Herdr
  {{ end -}}
  \```

### 2. Init prompt — `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl`

Add after `installSshServer` (line 27). **The prompt TEXT is the CI join key —
copy it verbatim into `windows.yml` (invariant #1); no `=` in the text.**

\```toml
    installHerdr         = {{ promptBoolOnce . "installHerdr" "Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)" false }}
\```

### 3. CI flags — `.github/workflows/windows.yml`

Add to the `$flags` array (after the sshd line, ~line 60), text matching #2 exactly:

\```
      '--promptBool','Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)=false',
\```

### 4. PATH + config env — `dot_config/powershell/profile.d/00_env.ps1`

- Add herdr's bin to the `$UserPaths` array (lines 48-51) so `herdr` resolves in
  interactive shells immediately; the existing `Where-Object { Test-Path }`
  filter makes it a clean no-op when herdr isn't installed:

  \```powershell
      (Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA 'Programs\Herdr\bin'),
  \```

- Add `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH` next to the other XDG file-redirects (near line 20-22,
  by `STARSHIP_CONFIG`):

  \```powershell
  # herdr reads its config here instead of %APPDATA%\herdr\config.toml (XDG-tidy).
  $env:HERDR_CONFIG_PATH = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'herdr/config.toml'
  \```

### 5. Managed config — new file `dot_config/herdr/config.toml`

Static TOML (no templating needed), deployed to `~/.config/herdr/config.toml`:

\```toml
# herdr — native Windows terminal multiplexer (preview beta).
# Managed by chezmoi; read via $env:HERDR_CONFIG_PATH (profile.d/00_env.ps1).
# Full default: `herdr --default-config`. Docs: https://herdr.dev/docs/configuration/

[update]
# Windows builds are preview-only; the installer rejects "stable" here.
channel = "preview"

[terminal]
# Match the rest of this repo (WezTerm / Windows Terminal default to pwsh).
default_shell = "pwsh"

[ui]
# "auto" draws herdr's own cursor on Windows to avoid ConPTY flicker;
# set "native" to hand the cursor to the outer terminal.
host_cursor = "auto"
\```

### 6. Gate the config file — `.chezmoiignore`

`.chezmoiignore` ignores `docs/**` etc. but not `.config/**`, so the file would
deploy for everyone. `.chezmoiignore` is rendered as a Go template, so gate it on
the toggle (append):

\```
{{ if not .installHerdr }}
.config/herdr/config.toml
{{ end }}
\```

### 7. Docs + skill (bilingual mirrors, same commit)

- **`docs/rationale.md:64-83` + `docs/rationale.zh-TW.md:55-71`** — update the
  multiplexer section: add a **herdr** row to the comparison table (native
  Windows, splits/tabs + detach/persist, "beta/preview") and a short paragraph
  noting herdr is now the native tmux-like option (opt-in via `installHerdr`)
  while **WezTerm stays the stable default**. Soften the "no native Windows
  builds" framing so it no longer reads as absolute.
- **`docs/tools.md:125-134` + `docs/tools.zh-TW.md:121-130`** — add a row to the
  "Opt-in dev stacks" table:
  `| herdr | native Windows terminal multiplexer (preview beta), installed via herdr.dev's install script; config at ~/.config/herdr/config.toml |`
- **`docs/setup.md:31-48` + `docs/setup.zh-TW.md:26-42`** — add an init-prompt
  table row: `| herdr multiplexer | off | native Windows terminal multiplexer (preview beta) |`
- **`.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md`** — add `· herdr: {{ .installHerdr }}`
  to the "What's enabled" line (line 56) and update the tmux/zellij Gotcha
  (lines 69-71) to mention herdr as the native beta multiplexer.
- **`CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` "Config surfaces" line** (~line 54) — append herdr
  (`~/.config/herdr/config.toml` via `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH`) so the architecture map
  stays accurate. Minor; keeps future agents oriented.

## Files to change

| # | File | Change |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` | new `installHerdr` prompt (default false) |
| 2 | `.github/workflows/windows.yml` | matching `--promptBool` flag |
| 3 | `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl` | header note + `Install-Herdr` + gated call |
| 4 | `dot_config/powershell/profile.d/00_env.ps1` | PATH entry + `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH` |
| 5 | `dot_config/herdr/config.toml` | **new** managed config |
| 6 | `.chezmoiignore` | gate config on `installHerdr` |
| 7 | `docs/rationale.md` + `.zh-TW.md` | multiplexer section update |
| 8 | `docs/tools.md` + `.zh-TW.md` | opt-in dev stacks row |
| 9 | `docs/setup.md` + `.zh-TW.md` | init-prompt table row |
| 10 | `.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md` | "What's enabled" + Gotcha |
| 11 | `CLAUDE.md` (`AGENTS.md` symlink) | config-surfaces line |

## Verification

Off-Windows (this checkout) — the installer can't actually run here; the
`windows-latest` CI is the real gate. Validate structurally:

1. **Template renders + parses** (isolated apply idiom from CLAUDE.md). Init a
   throwaway state passing **every** prompt including the new one
   (`--promptBool 'Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)=true'`),
   then `chezmoi execute-template … < run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl > r.ps1`
   and parse with pwsh — confirm the `Install-Herdr` block is present and parses.
   Re-render with the flag `=false` and confirm the block is absent.
2. **Config gating** — with `installHerdr=false`, confirm `chezmoi apply
   --exclude=scripts` into the temp destination does **not** write
   `.config/herdr/config.toml`; with `=true`, confirm it does.
3. **Config validity** — parse `dot_config/herdr/config.toml` with Python
   `tomllib` (per invariant #3 spirit) to confirm valid TOML.
4. **Lint** — `Invoke-ScriptAnalyzer` on the changed `.ps1.tmpl` (Errors only).
5. **Docs** — `just docs-build` must stay green (`--strict`; bilingual twins).
6. **CI parity** — confirm the new `windows.yml` flag text is byte-identical to
   the `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` prompt text (otherwise `--no-tty` init hangs).

On a real Windows box (post-merge sanity): `chezmoi apply` with herdr enabled →
`herdr --version` resolves, new shell has `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin` on
PATH, and `herdr` reads `~/.config/herdr/config.toml` (pwsh as default shell).

## Out of scope (note as TODO if wanted later)

- A `profile.d/15_herdr.ps1` shell-integration fragment (herdr's live-cwd is
  handled by `[terminal] new_cwd`, and PATH/config are covered in `00_env.ps1`)
  — add only if herdr ships a `herdr init powershell` hook worth caching.

  handled by `[terminal] new_cwd`, and PATH/config are covered in `00_env.ps1`)
  — add only if herdr ships a `herdr init powershell` hook worth caching.

<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.claude/worktrees/herdr/dot_config/powershell/profile.d/00_env.ps1`

```text
18	if (-not $env:XDG_CACHE_HOME)  { $env:XDG_CACHE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.cache' }
19	
20	$env:STARSHIP_CONFIG = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'starship.toml'
21	# yazi looks in %APPDATA%\yazi\config by default; keep it XDG-consistent instead.
22	$env:YAZI_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'yazi'
23	
24	# yazi needs the MSYS `file.exe` (bundled with Git) for MIME detection; without
25	# it every entry errors "Cannot find `file` … Set it up per the Windows guide".
26	# scoop doesn't shim git's usr/bin, so point YAZI_FILE_ONE at file.exe directly;
27	# fall back to a system/winget Git install. First existing candidate wins.
28	if (-not $env:YAZI_FILE_ONE) {
29	    $fileOne = @(
30	        (Join-Path $HOME 'scoop/apps/git/current/usr/bin/file.exe'),
31	        'C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin\file.exe'
32	    ) | Where-Object { Test-Path -LiteralPath $_ } | Select-Object -First 1
33	    if ($fileOne) { $env:YAZI_FILE_ONE = $fileOne }
34	}
35	
36	# uv-managed Python: `uv python install --default` drops python/python3 here.
37	# ~/.local/bin is prepended to PATH below, so they win over the Windows Store
38	# `python.exe` app-execution-alias.
39	$env:UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR = Join-Path $HOME '.local/bin'
40	
41	# Default editor
42	if (Get-Command nvim -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) { $env:EDITOR = 'nvim' }
43	
44	# Prepend user bin dirs to PATH, idempotently. scoop already adds its shims to
45	# the persisted user PATH, but a fresh session inside chezmoi apply may not have
46	# it yet; ~/.local/bin carries uv tools + the uv-managed python. Normalize each
47	# entry to backslashes (mixed slashes can fail Windows command resolution).
48	$UserPaths = @(
49	    (Join-Path $HOME '.local/bin'),
50	    (Join-Path $HOME 'scoop/shims')
51	) | Where-Object { $_ -and (Test-Path $_) } |
52	    ForEach-Object { [System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath($_) } | Select-Object -Unique
53	
54	$SepPaths = $env:PATH -split ';'
55	foreach ($p in $UserPaths) {
56	    if ($SepPaths -notcontains $p) { $env:PATH = "$p;$env:PATH" }
57	}
```
</details></tool-use>

```toml
# herdr — native Windows terminal multiplexer (preview beta).
# Managed by chezmoi; read via $env:HERDR_CONFIG_PATH (profile.d/00_env.ps1).
# Full default: `herdr --default-config`. Docs: https://herdr.dev/docs/configuration/

[update]
# Windows builds are preview-only; the installer rejects "stable" here.
channel = "preview"

[terminal]
# Match the rest of this repo (WezTerm / Windows Terminal default to pwsh).
default_shell = "pwsh"

[ui]
# "auto" draws herdr's own cursor on Windows to avoid ConPTY flicker;
# set "native" to hand the cursor to the outer terminal.
host_cursor = "auto"

```

```text
64	## Terminal multiplexer: WezTerm, not tmux/zellij
65	
66	`tmux` and `zellij` are **Unix-only** — there are no native Windows builds — so
67	they're absent here (you can still run them inside WSL). For a native tmux-like
68	experience on Windows the options are:
69	
70	| Option | Splits / tabs | Detach + persist | Notes |
71	|---|---|---|---|
72	| **WezTerm** | yes (panes/tabs, Lua `wezterm.mux`) | yes (mux server + `wezterm connect`) | native Windows; closest to tmux |
73	| Windows Terminal | yes (panes/tabs) | no | great terminal, no session persistence |
74	| Alacritty | no | no | fast, minimal; pair with a multiplexer |
75	| tmux / zellij | — | — | Unix-only; use under WSL |
76	
77	We install **WezTerm** (`wez.wezterm`) as the multiplexer answer: a native
78	Windows terminal with a built-in multiplexer — split panes, tabs, and a
79	persistent mux server you can detach from and reconnect to (`wezterm connect`),
80	plus scriptable layouts via its Lua `wezterm.mux` API. It isn't a 1:1 tmux (the
81	session/persistence model differs), but it's the closest native experience.
82	Windows Terminal stays installed for its panes/tabs; Alacritty stays as the fast
83	minimal option.
84	
85	## starship, not oh-my-posh
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
55	## 終端多工器：WezTerm，而非 tmux/zellij
56	
57	`tmux` 與 `zellij` 是**只有 Unix** —— 沒有原生 Windows 版 —— 所以此處沒有（你仍可在
58	WSL 裡跑）。要在 Windows 上取得類 tmux 體驗，選項是：
59	
60	| 選項 | 分割/分頁 | 卸離+保留 | 備註 |
61	|---|---|---|---|
62	| **WezTerm** | 有（panes/tabs、Lua `wezterm.mux`） | 有（mux server + `wezterm connect`） | 原生 Windows；最接近 tmux |
63	| Windows Terminal | 有（panes/tabs） | 無 | 很好的終端，但無 session 保留 |
64	| Alacritty | 無 | 無 | 快、極簡；搭配多工器用 |
65	| tmux / zellij | — | — | 只有 Unix；在 WSL 裡用 |
66	
67	我們安裝 **WezTerm**（`wez.wezterm`）作為多工器的答案：原生 Windows 終端、內建多工器
68	—— 分割 panes、分頁，以及可卸離再重連的常駐 mux server（`wezterm connect`），還能用
69	Lua `wezterm.mux` API 腳本化版面。它不是 1:1 的 tmux（session/保留模型不同），但已是
70	最接近的原生體驗。Windows Terminal 保留給 panes/tabs；Alacritty 保留為快速極簡選項。
71

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-11 00:31:02Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -uo pipefail
SRC="/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.claude/worktrees/herdr"
PKG=".chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl"

common_flags=(
  --promptChoice 'Role: workstation (full desktop) or minimal (shell only)=minimal'
  --promptString 'Your full name (git)=T'
  --promptString 'Your git email=t@e.com'
  --promptBool 'Install coding agents (Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex, Copilot CLI, SpecStory)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install GUI apps via winget (VSCode, Cursor, Notepad++, Terminal, Alacritty, PowerToys, Raycast)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install utility apps via winget (CPU-Z, GPU-Z, TreeSize, VLC, Everything, ShareX, HWiNFO)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install gaming apps (Steam)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install extra runtimes (rust, go, ruby) beyond node/bun/uv=false'
  --promptBool 'Install media CLIs (ffmpeg, imagemagick)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install local LLM tools (Ollama, LiteLLM)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install tunnel tools (ngrok, cloudflared)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install Infrastructure-as-Code tools (Azure CLI, Terraform, OpenTofu)=false'
  --promptBool 'Install and enable the OpenSSH server / sshd (needs admin, opens inbound port 22)=false'
  --promptBool 'Use China (GFW) mirrors for pip/npm/cargo/go/node=false'
  --promptBool 'Managed or corporate machine (skip org-policy-blocked apps like Tailscale and Grammarly)=false'
  --promptChoice 'Back up existing files before apply (smart|full|off)=off'
  --promptBool 'Enable PSReadLine vi editing mode=true'
)

run_case() {
  local herdr="$1"
  local TMPD; TMPD=$(mktemp -d); mkdir -p "$TMPD/home"
  local cfg="$TMPD/c.toml" db="$TMPD/s.db"
  echo "===================== installHerdr=$herdr ====================="
  chezmoi init --source="$SRC" --config="$cfg" --persistent-state="$db" \
    --destination="$TMPD/home" --no-tty \
    "${common_flags[@]}" \
    --promptBool "Install herdr (native Windows terminal multiplexer, preview beta)=$herdr" \
    >/dev/null 2>"$TMPD/init.err" || { echo "INIT FAILED:"; cat "$TMPD/init.err"; return 1; }

  # 1) render packages template + parse with pwsh
  chezmoi execute-template --config="$cfg" --source="$SRC" < "$SRC/$PKG" > "$TMPD/r.ps1" 2>"$TMPD/tmpl.err" \
    || { echo "TEMPLATE FAILED:"; cat "$TMPD/tmpl.err"; return 1; }
  pwsh -NoProfile -Command "\$null = [System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseFile('$TMPD/r.ps1',[ref]\$null,[ref]\$e); if (\$e){\$e|%{Write-Host \$_}; exit 1} else {Write-Host 'parse OK'}" \
    || { echo "PARSE FAILED"; return 1; }
  if grep -q 'function Install-Herdr' "$TMPD/r.ps1"; then echo "Install-Herdr func: PRESENT"; else echo "Install-Herdr func: MISSING ❌"; fi
  if grep -qx 'Install-Herdr' "$TMPD/r.ps1"; then echo "Install-Herdr call: PRESENT"; else echo "Install-Herdr call: ABSENT"; fi

  # 2) config-gating: apply files only, check ~/.config/herdr/config.toml
  chezmoi apply --source="$SRC" --config="$cfg" --persistent-state="$db" \
    --destination="$TMPD/home" --exclude=scripts >/dev/null 2>"$TMPD/apply.err" \
    || { echo "APPLY had errors:"; cat "$TMPD/apply.err"; }
  if [ -f "$TMPD/home/.config/herdr/config.toml" ]; then echo "config deployed: YES"; else echo "config deployed: NO"; fi
  rm -rf "$TMPD"
  echo
}

run_case true
run_case false

```

feat(packages): add herdr terminal multiplexer (opt-in, preview beta)

herdr is a native Windows terminal multiplexer (mouse-first panes/splits,
persistent sessions, detach/attach) — the first native answer to the
tmux/zellij gap called out in docs/rationale.md. It's beta / preview-only /
unsigned, so it ships behind a new opt-in `installHerdr` toggle (default off).

- Install: no scoop/winget/npm manifest, so a bespoke `Install-Herdr` helper
  runs herdr.dev's `irm|iex` bootstrap (modeled on Ensure-Scoop), guarded by
  the real install path and Register-Failure so it never aborts the apply.
- PATH + config: 00_env.ps1 puts %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin on PATH and
  points HERDR_CONFIG_PATH at ~/.config/herdr/config.toml (XDG, like
  STARSHIP_CONFIG). Managed config sets channel=preview + default_shell=pwsh;
  gated in .chezmoiignore so it only deploys when installHerdr is on.
- Wiring mirrors (same commit): new prompt in .chezmoi.toml.tmpl + matching CI
  flag in windows.yml, docs/setup + docs/tools (bilingual) rows, rationale
  multiplexer section, skill "What's enabled" + Gotcha, AGENTS.md surfaces.

Verified off-Windows: isolated init/render/parse (0 parse errors; func+call+
config present with herdr=on, call+config absent with herdr=off), config TOML
parses, CI prompt text byte-identical, PSScriptAnalyzer 0 errors, Pester 7/7,
mkdocs --strict clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
MSG

## What

Adds **herdr** — a native Windows terminal multiplexer (mouse-first panes/splits, persistent sessions, detach/attach) — as an **opt-in** tool with managed config. It's the first native answer to the tmux/zellij gap documented in `docs/rationale.md:64`.

herdr is beta / preview-only / unsigned on Windows, so it ships behind a new `installHerdr` init toggle (**default off**).

## Why

`docs/rationale.md` previously asserted there was *no* native Windows terminal multiplexer (hence WezTerm as the closest answer). herdr's experimental Windows beta fills that gap; WezTerm stays the stable default, herdr is the opt-in native tmux-like option.

## How

- **Install** — herdr has no scoop/winget/npm manifest, so a bespoke `Install-Herdr` helper runs herdr.dev's `irm | iex` bootstrap (modeled on the repo's own `Ensure-Scoop`), guarded by the real install path (`%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Herdr\bin\herdr.exe`) and `Register-Failure` so it never aborts the apply (invariant #2). Windows is preview-only (`$env:HERDR_CHANNEL='preview'`).
- **PATH + config** — `00_env.ps1` puts herdr's bin on PATH and points `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH` at `~/.config/herdr/config.toml` (XDG, exactly like `STARSHIP_CONFIG`). Managed config sets `channel="preview"` + `default_shell="pwsh"`; gated in `.chezmoiignore` so it only deploys when `installHerdr` is on.
- **Wiring mirrors (same commit)** — new prompt in `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` + matching CI flag in `windows.yml` (invariant #1), bilingual `docs/setup` + `docs/tools` rows, rationale multiplexer section, skill "What's enabled" + Gotcha, `AGENTS.md` config surfaces.

## Verification (off-Windows; `windows-latest` CI is the real gate)

- Isolated init → render → parse: **0 parse errors**; with `installHerdr=true` the `Install-Herdr` call + config are present/deployed, with `false` both are absent. ✅
- `dot_config/herdr/config.toml` parses via `tomllib`; CI prompt text byte-identical between `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` and `windows.yml`. ✅
- `Invoke-ScriptAnalyzer -Recurse`: **0 Errors**. Pester: **7/7**. `mkdocs build --strict`: clean (anchors fixed). ✅

## Notes

- The installer can't run in this checkout or in CI (`--exclude scripts`); real end-to-end (`herdr --version`, config read) needs a Windows box.
- Out of scope: a `profile.d/15_herdr.ps1` shell-integration fragment (live-cwd is handled by herdr's `[terminal] new_cwd`; PATH/config are covered in `00_env.ps1`).

)" 2>&1 | tail -5

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-11 00:42:46Z)**_

好问题 — 你的直觉基本对,但这不是我临时决定的,而是**这次是以「后台 job」在跑**,harness 对后台 job 有强制规则:

**1. 隔离(worktree)—— 主因正是你猜的**
后台 job 的启动说明里写死了:「在改动任何代码前,用 EnterWorktree 把工作从**其他并行 job** 和**用户的工作副本**里隔离出来」,而且是**强制**的 —— 在共享 checkout 里直接编辑文件会被拒绝(这次就是我写 plan 文件时撞到这个 guard,才进的 worktree)。原因就是:
- 可能有**别的 agent job 同时**在同一个 checkout 上跑,共用一棵工作树会互相覆盖;
- 你自己可能正在 main checkout 上编辑,不该被我搅乱;
- 连 `git stash` 栈都是跨 worktree 共享的,顺序 commit 到共享 main 确实容易踩到彼此。

所以不是「不方便 sequential commit」那么简单,而是「共享工作树下**并发**写会直接冲突」。worktree = 每个 job 一份独立的 repo 副本 + 独立分支。

**2. push + draft PR —— 是后台 job 的交付约定**
说明里也写了:一旦隔离到 worktree,「交付就是任务的一部分 —— commit、push 分支、开 draft PR,不用停下来问」。因为改动落在独立分支上,PR 是把它**摊给你 review + 让 CI 先跑一遍**再进 main 的自然方式。配套的硬规则是「绝不自动 push/merge 到 main、绝不 force-push」—— 所以 draft PR 就是那个「交给人来拍板」的交接点(也就是刚才我不肯替你 merge 的原因)。

**这不是唯一模式。** 前台交互式会话、或在你自己的 checkout 里跑时,我不会强推 worktree+PR,通常就按你的偏好开分支或原地改。如果你觉得对单人顺序开发太重,有两个出口:
- 在 `.claude/settings.json` 里设 `"worktree": {"bgIsolation": "none"}` —— 关掉这次 repo 的后台隔离强制,我就能直接在 checkout 里改;
- 或者干脆用前台交互模式跑,而不是丢成后台 job。

要我现在帮你把 `bgIsolation: none` 写进 `.claude/settings.json` 吗?(那之后本仓库的后台 job 就会直接在主 checkout 上顺序 commit,不再自动开 worktree。)

```text
> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Run parallel sessions with worktrees

> Isolate parallel Claude Code sessions in separate git worktrees so changes don't collide. Covers the `--worktree` flag, subagent isolation, `.worktreeinclude`, cleanup, and non-git VCS hooks.

A [git worktree](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree) is a separate working directory with its own files and branch, sharing the same repository history and remote as your main checkout. Running each Claude Code session in its own worktree means edits in one session never touch files in another, so you can have Claude building a feature in one terminal while fixing a bug in a second.

This page covers worktree isolation in the CLI. Everything below assumes a git repository. For other version control systems, see [Non-git version control](#non-git-version-control). The [desktop app](/en/desktop#work-in-parallel-with-sessions) creates a worktree for every new session automatically.

Worktrees are one of several ways to run Claude in parallel. They isolate file edits, while [subagents](/en/sub-agents) and [agent teams](/en/agent-teams) coordinate the work itself. See [Run agents in parallel](/en/agents) to compare the approaches, or skip ahead to [Isolate subagents with worktrees](#isolate-subagents-with-worktrees) to use worktrees and subagents together.

## Start Claude in a worktree

Pass `--worktree` or `-w` to create an isolated worktree and start Claude in it. By default, the worktree is created under `.claude/worktrees/<value>/` at your repository root, on a new branch named `worktree-<value>`:

```bash theme={null}
claude --worktree feature-auth
```

To put worktrees somewhere else, configure a [`WorktreeCreate` hook](#non-git-version-control). Run the command again with a different name in another terminal to start a second isolated session:

```bash theme={null}
claude --worktree bugfix-123
```

If you omit the name, Claude generates one such as `bright-running-fox`:

```bash theme={null}
claude --worktree
```

You can also ask Claude to "work in a worktree" during a session, and it will create one with the [`EnterWorktree`](/en/tools-reference) tool. Once in a worktree, Claude can switch directly to another one under `.claude/worktrees/` by calling `EnterWorktree` with the target path. The previous worktree stays on disk untouched.

{/* min-version: 2.1.198 */}As of v2.1.198, entering or exiting a worktree also relocates the session transcript to that directory's project storage, the same way [`/cd`](/en/commands) does, so `/desktop` and `--resume` find the session there afterward. Worktrees created by a [`WorktreeCreate` hook](#non-git-version-control) are excluded and keep the transcript at the launch directory.

Before using `--worktree` interactively in a directory for the first time, accept the workspace trust dialog by running `claude` once in that directory. If trust has not yet been accepted, `--worktree` exits with an error and prompts you to run `claude` in the directory first. Non-interactive runs with `-p` skip the [trust check](/en/security), so `claude -p --worktree` proceeds without it.

If Claude Code can't enter the worktree directory at startup, for example because a [`WorktreeCreate` hook](/en/hooks#worktreecreate) printed something other than the directory it created, or because the directory was deleted after it was set up, Claude Code prints an error naming the path and exits with code 1. Before v2.1.205, this crashed the session, and with `-p` it stalled for about 30 seconds before exiting with code 0.

{/* min-version: 2.1.200 */}Plugins installed at [project scope](/en/plugins-reference#plugin-installation-scopes) from the main checkout also load in worktrees of the same repository, so you don't need to reinstall them per worktree. This applies whether you create the worktree with `--worktree` or with `git worktree add`. Requires Claude Code v2.1.200 or later.

<Tip>
  Add `.claude/worktrees/` to your `.gitignore` so worktree contents don't appear as untracked files in your main checkout.
</Tip>

### Choose the base branch

Worktrees branch from your repository's default branch, `origin/HEAD`, so they start from a clean tree matching the remote. If no remote is configured or the fetch fails, the worktree falls back to your current local `HEAD`. To always branch from local `HEAD` instead, set `worktree.baseRef` to `"head"` in [settings](/en/settings#worktree-settings). Setting `baseRef` to `"head"` makes new worktrees carry your unpushed commits and feature-branch state, which is useful when isolating subagents that need to operate on in-progress work. The setting accepts only `"fresh"` or `"head"`, not arbitrary git refs:

```json theme={null}
{
  "worktree": {
    "baseRef": "head"
  }
}
```

To branch from a specific pull request, pass the PR number prefixed with `#`, or a full GitHub pull request URL. Claude Code fetches `pull/<number>/head` from `origin` and creates the worktree at `.claude/worktrees/pr-<number>`:

```bash theme={null}
claude --worktree "#1234"
```

For full control over how worktrees are created, configure a [`WorktreeCreate` hook](/en/hooks#worktreecreate), which replaces the default `git worktree` logic entirely.

## Copy gitignored files into worktrees

A worktree is a fresh checkout, so untracked files like `.env` or `.env.local` from your main repository are not present. To copy them automatically when Claude creates a worktree, add a `.worktreeinclude` file to your project root.

The file uses `.gitignore` syntax. Only files that match a pattern and are also gitignored are copied, so tracked files are never duplicated.

This `.worktreeinclude` copies two env files and a secrets config into each new worktree:

```text .worktreeinclude theme={null}
.env
.env.local
config/secrets.json
```

This applies to worktrees created with `--worktree`, [subagent worktrees](#isolate-subagents-with-worktrees), and parallel sessions in the [desktop app](/en/desktop#work-in-parallel-with-sessions).

## Isolate subagents with worktrees

Subagents can run in their own worktrees so parallel edits don't conflict. Ask Claude to "use worktrees for your agents", or set it permanently on a [custom subagent](/en/sub-agents#supported-frontmatter-fields) by adding `isolation: worktree` to the frontmatter. Each subagent gets a temporary worktree that is removed automatically when the subagent finishes without changes.

Subagent worktrees use the same [base branch](#choose-the-base-branch) as `--worktree`, so they branch from your repository's default branch unless `worktree.baseRef` is set to `"head"`.

## Clean up worktrees

When you exit a worktree session, cleanup depends on whether you made changes:

* **No uncommitted changes, no untracked files, and no new commits**: the worktree and its branch are removed automatically. If the session has a [name](/en/sessions#name-your-sessions), Claude prompts instead so you can keep the worktree for later
* **Uncommitted changes, untracked files, or new commits exist**: Claude prompts you to keep or remove the worktree. Keeping preserves the directory and branch so you can return later. Removing deletes the worktree directory and its branch, discarding any uncommitted changes, untracked files, and commits
* **Non-interactive runs**: worktrees created with `--worktree` alongside `-p` are not cleaned up automatically since there is no exit prompt. Remove them with `git worktree remove`

Worktrees that Claude created for subagents and [background sessions](/en/agent-view#how-file-edits-are-isolated) are removed automatically once they are older than your [`cleanupPeriodDays`](/en/settings#available-settings) setting, provided they have no uncommitted changes, no untracked files, and no unpushed commits. Worktrees you create with `--worktree` are never removed by this sweep.

While an agent is running, Claude runs `git worktree lock` on its worktree so that concurrent cleanup cannot remove it. The lock is released when the agent finishes. To clean up a worktree that the sweep keeps, run `git worktree remove`, adding `--force` if the worktree has uncommitted changes or untracked files.

On Windows, before removing a worktree, Claude Code removes any NTFS junction or directory symlink at any depth inside it as a link entry, so removing the worktree doesn't delete the files a link points to. Before v2.1.205, Claude Code removed only top-level links as link entries, and removing a worktree with a junction nested in a subdirectory could delete the contents of the directory the link pointed to outside the worktree.

## Manage worktrees manually

For full control over worktree location and branch configuration, create worktrees with Git directly. This is useful when you need to check out a specific existing branch or place the worktree outside the repository.

Create a worktree on a new branch:

```bash theme={null}
git worktree add ../project-feature-a -b feature-a
```

Create a worktree from an existing branch:

```bash theme={null}
git worktree add ../project-bugfix bugfix-123
```

Start Claude in the worktree:

```bash theme={null}
cd ../project-feature-a && claude
```

List your worktrees:

```bash theme={null}
git worktree list
```

Remove one when you're done with it:

```bash theme={null}
git worktree remove ../project-feature-a
```

See the [Git worktree documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree) for the full command reference. Remember to initialize your development environment in each new worktree: install dependencies, set up virtual environments, or run whatever your project's setup requires.

## Non-git version control

Worktree isolation uses git by default. For SVN, Perforce, Mercurial, or other systems, configure [`WorktreeCreate` and `WorktreeRemove` hooks](/en/hooks#worktreecreate) to provide custom creation and cleanup logic. Because the hook replaces the default git behavior, [`.worktreeinclude`](#copy-gitignored-files-into-worktrees) is not processed when you use `--worktree`. Copy any local configuration files inside your hook script instead.

This `WorktreeCreate` hook reads the worktree name from stdin, checks out a fresh SVN working copy, and prints the directory path so Claude Code can use it as the session's working directory:

```json theme={null}
{
  "hooks": {
    "WorktreeCreate": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash -c 'NAME=$(jq -r .name); DIR=\"$HOME/.claude/worktrees/$NAME\"; svn checkout https://svn.example.com/repo/trunk \"$DIR\" >&2 && echo \"$DIR\"'"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

Pair it with a `WorktreeRemove` hook to clean up when the session ends. See the [hooks reference](/en/hooks#worktreecreate) for the input schema and a removal example.

## See also

Worktrees handle file isolation. The related pages below cover delegating work into those isolated checkouts and switching between the sessions you create:

* [Subagents](/en/sub-agents): delegate work to isolated agents within a session
* [Agent teams](/en/agent-teams): coordinate multiple Claude sessions automatically
* [Manage sessions](/en/sessions): name, resume, and switch between conversations
* [Desktop parallel sessions](/en/desktop#work-in-parallel-with-sessions): worktree-backed sessions in the desktop app
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-view.md`

```text
Summary: agent view (claude agents)

What it is
- A terminal dashboard for dispatching and managing many background Claude Code sessions from one place. Each row is a full background session (keeps running without a terminal attached), shows state, recent output/summary, and lets you peek, reply, or attach to the full conversation.

How to open it
- From a shell:
  - claude agents
  - Scope to a project/directory:
    - claude agents --cwd ~/projects/my-app
- Start a background session directly from the shell:
  - claude --bg "investigate the flaky test"
  - claude --bg --name "flaky-test-fix" "investigate the flaky test"
- From inside a running session: /background or /bg moves it to agent view.

Core workflow (dispatch → monitor → attach → detach)
- Dispatch: type a prompt in the input line at the bottom of agent view and press Enter to start a new background session. Shift+Enter = dispatch and attach immediately.
- Peek & reply: select a row and press Space to open the peek panel (shows status or question). Type a reply and press Enter to send it without leaving agent view; Tab fills a suggested reply.
- Attach: press Enter or → on a row to attach to a session (full interactive session). Detach: press ← on an empty prompt, /exit, Ctrl+Z (returns to where you started), or double Ctrl+C/D as usual.
- Background from foreground: pressing ← on an empty prompt backgrounds the session and opens agent view (single press).

Keyboard shortcuts (most useful)
- ↑ / ↓ : move between rows
- Enter : attach to selected session (or dispatch if input has text)
- Space : open/close peek panel for the selected row
- Shift+Enter : dispatch and attach immediately
- → : attach
- ← : detach / background (on empty prompt)
- Tab : browse subagents on empty input or accept a suggestion
- Ctrl+S : toggle grouping between state and directory
- Ctrl+T : pin / unpin selected session (keeps its process running while idle)
- Ctrl+R : rename selected session
- Ctrl+X : stop session; press again within two seconds to delete it
- Shift+↑ / Shift+↓ : reorder sessions within a group
- ? : show all shortcuts
- Ctrl+G : open dispatch prompt in $VISUAL/$EDITOR

Scope: machine-wide vs project-specific
- Machine-wide list by default: agent view shows every background session you started (across all projects) on that user on this machine.
- Project-scoped: claude agents --cwd <path> limits the list to sessions started under that directory.
- Dispatch target: a new session runs in the directory where you opened agent view. To dispatch into another repo:
  - Open claude agents in that directory or
  - Mention a child repo/worktree with @<repo> in the prompt (lists one-level git repos and registered worktrees) or
  - Use claude --bg from that directory.

Managing concurrent sessions
- You can dispatch many sessions in parallel; each is its own Claude Code process managed by a per-user supervisor.
- Concurrency notes:
  - Each background session consumes model usage quota independently (rate limits / billing apply).
  - File edits: sessions isolate edits into git worktrees under .claude/worktrees/ by default, so parallel sessions can edit in isolation. You can disable worktree isolation per repo via worktree.bgIsolation = "none".
  - Pin sessions (Ctrl+T) to keep their process running while idle; otherwise supervisor may stop idle processes after ~1 hour (state is preserved on disk and the process is restarted on attach/peek/reply).
  - To stop/delete sessions from shell:
    - claude attach <id>
    - claude logs <id>
    - claude stop <id>
    - claude rm <id> (removes session; preserves worktree if it has uncommitted changes and prints path)
  - Supervisor handles handing off/resuming background work (shell commands, workflows, background subagents) if a session process is stopped or restarted.
  - Avoid dispatching many heavy sessions at once because each consumes resources and your provider quota.

Useful commands / examples
- Open agent view:
  - claude agents
  - claude agents --cwd ~/projects/my-app
- Run background session from shell:
  - claude --bg "run tests and fix failures"
  - claude --bg --name "ci-fixer" "run tests and fix failures"
- Run a shell command as a background job:
  - In agent view dispatch input prefix with !, e.g.: ! pytest -x
  - Or: claude --bg --exec 'pytest -x'
- Attach, inspect, stop:
  - claude attach 7c5dcf5d
  - claude logs 7c5dcf5d
  - claude stop 7c5dcf5d
  - claude rm 7c5dcf5d

Limitations & cautions
- Agent view is local (sessions run on your machine). Sessions stop when the machine shuts down (they survive sleep).
- Rate limits / usage: running many agents in parallel consumes quota accordingly.
- Deleting a session from the UI removes a Claude-created worktree and any uncommitted changes (use claude rm to keep worktree when needed).
- Some features are preview and may require recent Claude Code versions (check claude --version).

Short documentation excerpts (commands)
- Open agent view: claude agents
- Dispatch background session from shell: claude --bg "your prompt"
- Dispatch background shell job: type ! before command in agent view or use --exec
- Pin session: Ctrl+T
- Peek: Space
- Attach: Enter or →

If you want, I can show a step-by-step example: dispatch a session, peek and reply, then attach and view its worktree. Which would you like?
```
</details></tool-use>
